story:
I see my Rose as she was on our wedding day: amber eyes shining through her veil like sunlight through clouds, ebony hair falling in bold waves against her white gown, as fearless as her character and as deep as the night sky. My hands trembled; my knees wavered. Through happy tears I kissed her, and we promised to love each other until death. I pulled a single red rose from the altar vase. “A rose, for my most beautiful Rose. Know that you will always be my life.” She smiled, “Forever and always,” and kissed me again. That is the kiss I carry with me, the one that returns whenever I close my eyes.

After four radiant years, she was gone — to the one place I can’t follow. I know she’ll never answer a phone or read a letter. Still, I reach for her in the quiet hours and refuse to believe I cannot say “I love you” again. The thought of her lying cold and alone shatters me. God, I loved her.

Her absence tore a black hole through my soul. What is the point of living when the one who gave your life meaning is no longer here? I feel sick each day. Memory tightens around my heart like a fist wringing the life from a rag. I force myself to keep thinking of her, because to stop would feel like betrayal, like letting her slip away twice.

We had more than chemistry and friendship; we had a language without words. We saw each other’s flaws and loved each other anyway, magnifying the good until it outshone the rest. She completed me in ways that made the world feel orderly and bright.

The last time I saw her, she stormed out, angry. The door slammed; silence followed. I cannot even remember what we argued about—only the sound, the shock, the empty space she left behind. I blame myself for letting her drive while upset. I should have known. I should have stopped her. The guilt sits in my chest, heavy and constant.

Death ended our vows, but it did not end my love. Without her, the world has dimmed. I pray for her return even as I know such prayers cannot change what has happened. So I do the one thing I can: I visit her.

Every day for a year I have knelt by her grave with roses—pink like her tender lips, white like her soul, yellow like the golden flicker in her eyes, and mostly red like the heart that beat in time with mine. The stone calls her an incredible wife, but words chip and fade; they cannot hold everything she was. I press my hand to the cold name and tell her what I should have said every time we parted: I’m sorry. I love you. I always will. I will be back tomorrow. Goodbye, my Rose.
